title: Untitled
research_problem: Understanding pedestrian street-crossing behavior and modeling pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections during the red-man phase of traffic light signals
method_summary: The paper proposes a new statistical model for pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections. It starts from a bounded Pareto distribution for a specific pedestrian under given traffic conditions, then uses a generalized linear model to relate waiting time to vehicle time headway, and finally employs statistical unconditioning to derive the unconditional distribution accounting for vehicle-pedestrian interaction. This leads to a mixture distribution with four components that exhibits a U-shaped pattern.
datasets: Field data from signalized intersection in Kunming, China (283 valid observations, collected on two consecutive weekend days)

main_claims.claim_text: Pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections follow a U-shaped distribution, characterized by a large proportion crossing immediately and a large proportion waiting for the entire red-man phase
The proposed mixture distribution model with bounded Pareto components can reduce to J-shaped or L-shaped distributions under certain traffic scenarios

The model successfully differentiates between risk-taking and risk-averse pedestrians based on their effective critical headway and sensitivity to vehicle gaps, with practical implications for pedestrian safety
The commonly used exponential distribution and its extensions are inadequate for modeling bounded pedestrian waiting times at signalized intersections
Empirical analysis shows that younger pedestrians tend to be risk-takers while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse, with significant differences in waiting behavior across age and gender groups

limitations.text: The model focuses on univariate analysis with only waiting time as the explicit variable, limiting ability to simultaneously analyze multiple risk factors
The paper does not address how the model performs at different types of intersections or under varying traffic control schemes beyond the single case study
Parameter estimation relies on EM algorithm which may face identifiability issues common in mixture models, particularly when some components converge to degenerate distributions

future_work: Develop multivariate modeling approach for pedestrians' waiting times that can incorporate multiple risk factors simultaneously
Extend the model to account for multivariate relationships beyond normal or exponential distribution assumptions
Apply the model to evaluate impact of new traffic-light control schemes and roadway-layout redesigns
notes: The red-man phase duration in the study was 75 seconds with total signal cycle of 110 seconds; Key model parameters include effective critical headway (ECH) and sensitivity coefficient for excess headway; Risk-taking pedestrians have lower ECH while risk-averse pedestrians have higher ECH; Goodness-of-fit test (chi-square = 3.51) confirmed model validity at 5% significance level; Model was applied to analyze real field data from Kunming, China
